title: Chamomile infusion for tension headache
subtitle: An antispasmodic infusion for the headache that comes with stress or muscle tension
excerpt: A hot infusion of dried chamomile flowers for a tension-type headache, the dull, squeezing headache that comes with stress, poor posture, or fatigue. Chamomile's antispasmodic action on smooth and skeletal muscle makes it one of the tradition's first-response herbs for mild tension pain.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), chamomile entry, antispasmodic and nervine properties, use for headache and nervous conditions. Culpeper, The English Physician (1652), chamomile for pain and tension. EMA HMPC monograph on Matricaria flos (2015), antispasmodic and nervine traditional actions confirmed.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dried chamomile flowers keep 12 months in a sealed tin away from light. Brew each cup fresh.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb traditionally associated with easing muscle spasm, in the context of tension headache, this refers to the tight, contracted muscles of the neck and scalp.

content.text: A cup of chamomile for the tension headache that comes at the end of a stressful day, the dull, squeezing pain across the forehead and the back of the head that follows hours at a screen, emotional stress, or physical tension in the neck and shoulders. Chamomile's

action works on smooth and skeletal muscle; in this context it eases the muscular component of the tension pattern, the tight scalp and neck muscles that drive the headache and keep it going. The preparation is the same two-teaspoons-in-a-covered-pot formula as every other chamomile infusion: simple, consistent, and ready in twelve minutes.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Asteraceae allergy: chamomile is in the Asteraceae (daisy) family. Readers with a known allergy to ragweed, chrysanthemum, calendula, or related plants should avoid chamomile preparations.

This is a preparation for tension-type headache only, the dull, bilateral, non-throbbing type associated with stress and muscle tension. It is not appropriate for migraines, cluster headaches, or headaches that are severe or unusual.

See your doctor immediately for: the 'worst headache of your life', a headache with stiff neck and fever, a headache with sudden visual disturbance, weakness, or speech difficulty, or a headache that follows a head injury. See your GP for headaches that are getting progressively worse, headaches that wake you from sleep, or headaches that occur more than 15 days a month.

items.prepNote: about 2 heaped teaspoons of loose-leaf dried chamomile flowers

content.text: Place {{dried-chamomile-flowers}} of dried chamomile flowers in the teapot or infuser. Boil the kettle, wait 30 seconds, then pour {{water}} of just-off-boil water over. Put the lid on immediately. Steep for 10 minutes. Strain. Sit down, close your eyes, and drink the cup slowly. If the headache is also driving tension in the neck, a warm compress on the back of the neck while drinking the chamomile tea adds a second layer of relief.

One cup at the onset of the headache. A second cup after an hour if the first has not brought relief. Up to three cups during a single headache episode for adults. If the headache is not responding after two to three cups, or if it is getting worse, the chamomile tea is not the right response, take an appropriate analgesic or seek medical advice.
When NOT to use this remedy

Do not use for severe headaches, the worst headache you have ever had, headaches with neurological symptoms (visual changes, weakness, confusion, speech difficulty), headaches with fever and stiff neck, or headaches following a head injury. These all require emergency medical assessment. Skip chamomile if you have an Asteraceae allergy.
